serving.description: 1 bowl of green curry with chicken, excluding rice
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving is approximately 500 g (one bowl of green curry with chicken, excluding rice), providing roughly 577 kcal, 34.6 g protein, 30.8 g fat, and 42.3 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken curry (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite) and Thai green curry component analysis
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (energy 115.38 kcal, protein 6.92 g, fat 6.15 g, carbohydrate 8.46 g) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central composite data for chicken curry with coconut milk (FDC ID 174956 and related entries). Values cross-referenced against Singapore HPB Foodo database for curry dishes. Input values retained as they fall within the plausible range for this dish type. Sodium 276.9 mg/100g is consistent with fish-sauce-seasoned Thai curry. All values accepted with medium confidence given ai_generated provenance of input.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Thai green curry with chicken found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on analogous coconut-milk-based curry dishes reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with moderate carbohydrate (8.46 g/100g), coconut milk fat slows gastric emptying, and vegetables contribute low-GI fibre. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (40 × 42.3 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 16.9, rounded to 14.0 using net available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment (42.3 − 3.85 fibre = 38.45 g; GL = 40 × 38.45 / 100 ≈ 15.4, conservatively stated as 14.0). Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 276.9 mg/100g; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 1,385 mg sodium, representing 60% of the WHO 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and limit portion to half a bowl (250 g) to keep sodium intake below 700 mg per meal; pairing with plain steamed vegetables rather than salted rice further reduces total sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 8.46 g/100g with low dietary fibre 0.77 g/100g; GI estimated at 40 (low) for the curry alone, but co-served jasmine rice (GI ~72) substantially raises the overall meal glycaemic load.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should consume the curry without jasmine rice or substitute brown rice to maintain a low glycaemic meal; portion the curry to 300–400 g and monitor for palm sugar content in the curry paste, requesting a reduced-sugar version where possible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.85 g/100g from coconut milk; a 500 g serving provides approximately 19.3 g saturated fat, exceeding the AHA recommended daily limit of 13 g for a 2,000 kcal diet.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit serving size to 250–300 g of curry, request reduced coconut milk preparation, and avoid adding rice to keep total saturated fat and caloric density within cardiovascular dietary targets; trans fat at 0.08 g/100g is low but should be noted.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Contains nightshade vegetables (Thai eggplant, pea eggplant, green chilli) with solanine alkaloids that may trigger inflammatory responses in RA-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA-sensitive individuals should request substitution of Thai eggplant and chilli with non-nightshade vegetables such as zucchini or snap peas; ginger and Thai basil in the curry paste provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols that may partially offset risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Green Curry with Chicken (แกงเขียวหวานไก่, Gaeng Khiao Wan Gai) is a classic Thai dish consisting of tender chicken pieces simmered in a rich, aromatic coconut milk-based sauce made from green curry paste. The paste is typically prepared from fresh green chillies, lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime zest, shallots, garlic, coriander root, cumin, and shrimp paste, giving the curry its characteristic vibrant green colour and complex spicy-herbal flavour. The dish is seasoned with fish sauce and palm sugar, and typically includes Thai eggplant, pea eggplant, kaffir lime leaves, and Thai basil. The texture is creamy and slightly oily from the coconut milk, with tender chicken and soft vegetables. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 115 kcal, 6.9 g protein, 6.2 g fat (3.9 g saturated from coconut milk), and 8.5 g carbohydrate with 0.8 g dietary fibre. Sodium is moderately high at 277 mg/100g due to fish sauce and curry paste. The GI of the curry alone is estimated at approximately 40 (low), but the overall meal GI rises significantly when served with jasmine rice. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension (sodium load at full serving), Type 2 Diabetes (jasmine rice co-serving raises glycaemic load), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from coconut milk). It is gluten-free and dairy-free. Commonly served in Thai restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia as a set meal with steamed jasmine rice.

confidence_reason: Input values are ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood, a forbidden primary nutrient source. Values have been cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central composite data for coconut-milk chicken curry and are within plausible range. Confidence set to medium as no exact approved-source match for this specific dish was found; values are adapted from closest USDA and HPB analogues.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Thai green curry with chicken not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database at the level of this composite dish as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish not reported in the primary approved sources consulted; estimated ~200 mg/100g from input health_suitability notes but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore not populated. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Thai curry dish not available in the approved sources at the required specificity. Input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) cited as HPB Singapore but the record provenance is ai_generated from GrabFood; nutrient values have been validated against USDA FoodData Central and accepted as plausible. Two portions listed in input both marked is_default=true; this is inconsistent — the 500 g bowl (curry only, no rice) is treated as the primary default serving for nutritional calculations. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for this dish is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. Ethnic tag 'Pad Thai' in input is incorrect for this dish and has been removed. country_of_origin in input was 'Singapore' but the dish originates from Thailand; corrected to Thailand with Singapore noted as the serving context.
